rafial: Wow, that's quite a story. For my part, AlphaGo filled me with excitement when it happened, I remember watching the live stream and being so impressed with how Michael Redmond was able to explain what was happening in a way that I could follow and understand.
rafial: I never had any worry for Go as a game. Chess still thrives, despite computer chess having surpassed human grandmasters quite awhile ago.
rafial: At the end of the day, I was never going to be playing anywhere near top level Go, so for me, AlphaGo just showed us there are levels to the game that we as human players have yet to explore.

rafial: During my dedicated time playing Go, I mostly played at the Go Center here in Seattle. We are lucky to have one of the few remaining Go Centers originally crated by Iwamoto Sensei, and it has a thriving community.
rafial: I zoomed up about 12k, then made steady progress to 8k. And at that point I stalled, I just didn't have the time or will to put in the sustained study I would need to get stronger.
rafial: At some point, I just burned out.

rafial: Part of the difficulty for me is that I think I hit that level where I wasn't good enough to play interesting games with folks who are solely dedicated to the game. And I was getting too good to play interesting game with folks who are just casually interested. You can only play so many teaching games.

rafial: And I much prefer to play with people I know (even on line) as for me part of joy of the game is camaraderie over the board. As you alluded to up above, the very competitive online scene seems to breed poor behaviors, and I always got the impression that there was an emphasis on speed play.
rafial: Anyway, I should make a move.

rafial: I do think you are little stronger than me, I certainly feel like I'm holding on by my fingertips. All for the good to be challenged at a level where you can still understand what is happening.
